name: Cooldown Time
description: A reusable action becomes unavailable after activation, then returns after a tracked recharge interval.
design_notes: Cooldowns are pacing contracts: the player spends an action now and plans around the next available window. The useful design work is deciding which gameplay_clock_id owns the recharge, whether charges recover independently, how duplicate activation is rejected, and how the UI communicates readiness without encouraging constant staring at icons.
design_purpose: Prevent high-impact actions from being repeated every input frame.
Create readable windows where enemies, objectives, or traversal routes can pressure the player.
Give designers a pacing lever that is easier to reason about than hidden probability or opaque resource drains.
player_fantasy: The player feels powerful when an ability comes back exactly when their plan needs it.

parameters.name: recharge_interval_ms
parameters.description: Milliseconds between a confirmed activation and the next accepted activation for the same ability slot.

parameters.name: recharge_group_id
parameters.description: Identifier shared by actions that should lock each other out after one of them succeeds.

parameters.name: charge_capacity
parameters.description: Maximum stored activations before the recharge scheduler stops granting additional charges.

parameters.name: started_at_server_ms
parameters.description: Authority timestamp recorded when the accepted activation begins its recharge interval.

parameters.name: pause_policy_id
parameters.description: Rule that decides whether the recharge clock advances while the game is paused or loading.

edge_cases: An activation request arrives twice from buffered input, rollback resimulation, or a delayed network packet.
The player saves, loads, dies, or changes equipment while one or more charges are partially recovered.
A pause menu uses unscaled_time while the recharge scheduler uses gameplay_clock_id, causing UI and authority state to disagree.
Two abilities share recharge_group_id but one is removed from the character during the lockout window.
common_bugs: The UI displays zero remaining milliseconds before the authority state accepts a new activation.
Recharge progress resets when the owning entity is pooled, respawned, or migrated to a different network owner.
Client prediction grants an action locally, then the server rejects it because started_at_server_ms was stale.
Multiple charges recover simultaneously when only the oldest spent charge should be advancing.
balancing_notes: Short intervals make the action part of the base rhythm; long intervals turn it into a deliberate commitment.
Shared lockouts are useful for preventing optimal rotations, but they can make loadouts feel samey if every strong tool blocks every other tool.
Recharge indicators should reveal enough timing to plan without requiring frame-perfect input to get full value.
accessibility_notes: Pair radial fills with numeric or icon state changes so readiness is not communicated only by color or motion.
Allow input buffering near the final recharge window for players who cannot repeatedly tap at exact readiness frames.

implementation_notes.unity: Store recharge_started_at_ms, charge_count, and recharge_group_id in the ability model rather than deriving them from MonoBehaviour lifetime. Use Time.timeAsDouble for local prediction, a server timestamp for multiplayer authority, and drive UI Toolkit or Canvas indicators from a read-only remaining_ms query.
implementation_notes.godot: Keep recharge state in a Resource or autoloaded ability registry with a monotonic gameplay_clock_id. Process acceptance in _physics_process for deterministic input order, but update Control nodes from a projected remaining_ms so UI remains smooth while pause_policy_id is respected.
implementation_notes.unreal: Gameplay Ability System cooldown effects can own the authoritative lockout, but still store cooldown_group_id and charge metadata for UI and save restoration. For replicated games, reject duplicate activation in the server ability commit path and let UMG display predicted progress until correction arrives.
implementation_notes.web: Use performance.now or a fixed simulation tick as the local clock, but keep activation acceptance in one transaction that writes started_at_ms, charge_count, and group locks together. Phaser, Canvas, or custom UI should render projected remaining_ms without mutating the ability state.

relationships.reason: Cooldown systems need visible remaining-window feedback for planning, input timing, and accessibility.
Reload windows can share cooldown timing state for ready checks and duplicate activation prevention.
Dash cooldowns limit repeated burst movement and create recovery windows between evasive actions.

example_games.title: World of Warcraft
example_games.note: Uses ability recharge and shared lockouts across many combat actions.

example_games.title: Hades
example_games.note: Uses short recovery windows to pace repeated combat and movement actions.
